items.q: What are the 4 Cs of food hygiene?
items.correct: Cleaning, Cooking, Chilling, and avoiding Cross-contamination.
items.why: Students guess at the Cs. They specifically address the four food-safety risks: dirty surfaces, undercooked food, warm storage, and pathogens transferring between foods (raw to ready-to-eat).

items.q: On a traffic-light label, what does a red light indicate?
items.correct: High amounts of that nutrient per 100g - eat less often.
items.misconception: The food is dangerous and should never be eaten.
items.why: Students treat red as 'forbidden'. Traffic lights are a guidance system - red = high, eat less often or in small portions. Many foods (cheese, olive oil) are red on certain nutrients but fine in moderation.

items.q: What temperature range is the food 'danger zone'?
items.correct: 5 to 63 degrees Celsius - where most bacteria multiply rapidly.
items.why: Students assume danger = very hot. It's the MIDDLE range that's dangerous because bacteria thrive there. Fridges stay below 5; cooking exceeds 63 (often 75+ for safety). The middle is where they multiply.

items.correct: No - vegetarians eat no meat or fish; someone who eats fish but not meat is a pescatarian.
items.misconception: Yes - fish doesn't count as meat, so vegetarians can eat it.
items.random_wrong: Vegetarians eat fish only at weekends.
items.why: A very common mix-up. Vegetarians avoid all animal flesh including fish and shellfish (and by-products of slaughter such as gelatine). The fish-eating diet has its own name: pescatarian.

items.q: Is honey or brown sugar a 'healthy' substitute for white sugar?
items.correct: Not really - they are all free sugars with similar energy content and the same effect on teeth.
items.misconception: Honey and brown sugar are natural, so they don't count as sugar.
items.why: Students assume 'natural' or 'brown' means healthy. Honey, syrups and brown sugar count towards free-sugar intake just like white sugar - similar kcal per gram and the same link to tooth decay. Trace minerals in brown sugar are nutritionally insignificant.

items.q: How many portions of fruit and veg a day are recommended?
items.why: It's 5. “10” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Eatwell Guide is from which country?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: UK. “USA” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Recommended portions of oily fish per week?
items.why: The real figure is 1 portion. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
